title: Andys Play

jokes.text: Did you write this? - No. - Who did? - Stephen sondheim. - Who is he?
jokes.setup: Office workers encountering sophisticated musical theater
jokes.punchline: Complete ignorance of Stephen Sondheim, a legendary composer
jokes.explanation: Simple culture clash humor - office workers vs. high art. Economical delivery but predictable setup.

dialogue.speaker: Unknown
dialogue.line: Who is Stephen Sondheim?

dialogue.speaker: Unknown
dialogue.line: He's only like the greatest musical theater composer of all time.

dialogue.speaker: Unknown
dialogue.line: Oh. I thought you said 'Sonny in the Attic' or something.

jokes.text: What the hell is happening?
jokes.setup: Musical theater performance in office setting
jokes.punchline: Perfectly captures audience confusion at the absurdity
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy and timing - captures the absurdity in four words. Relatable reaction beat.

dialogue.speaker: Michael Scott
dialogue.line: Welcome everyone to our office production of... Sweeney Todd!

dialogue.speaker: Dwight Schrute
dialogue.line: I will be playing the barber with historically accurate precision.

dialogue.speaker: Jim Halpert
dialogue.line: Why are we doing Sweeney Todd in the office?

dialogue.speaker: Michael Scott
dialogue.line: Because it's a classic! And it teaches us about trust and betrayal in the workplace.

dialogue.speaker: Pam Beesly
dialogue.line: Michael, there's a meat grinder on stage.

dialogue.speaker: Michael Scott
dialogue.line: It's a PROP meat grinder, Pam. Relax.

jokes.text: a wee bit of viral marketing, gov'nor!
jokes.setup: Explaining the musical performance interruption
jokes.punchline: Over-the-top theatrical accent and antiquated slang
jokes.explanation: Classic Andy theatrical overcommitment. Character-specific cringe humor.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I was performing a number from The Music Man, and I got so into character, I couldn't break out of it. I kept saying things like 'Well, I'll be hornswoggled!' and 'That's the berries!' My whole face was doing this weird thing.

jokes.text: I auditioned for this! When did the cast list go up? Like a month ago. Really? They didn't call me!
jokes.setup: Andy's excitement about the performance
jokes.punchline: Revelation that he auditioned but wasn't cast, delivered publicly
jokes.explanation: Perfect cringe setup - Andy's public humiliation. Good structure building to the rejection reveal.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I'm so excited about this performance tonight! I've been practicing my lines all week.

dialogue.speaker: Cast member
dialogue.line: Andy, the performance is tonight. Did you get cast?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Well, actually... I auditioned. But they went with someone else.

jokes.text: Who am I playing?
jokes.setup: Andy still thinks he's in the play after learning the cast list went up
jokes.punchline: His continued delusion in the face of obvious rejection
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy - three words capture Andy's complete lack of self-awareness. Excellent cringe escalation.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Guys, did you see the cast list went up?

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Yeah, congratulations on not making it.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: What? No, I'm definitely in it. I can feel it. The director just... he's probably waiting to announce the leads separately. Very common in theatre.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Andy, your name isn't on there.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: That's a typo. Has to be. I nailed that audition. Did you see me? I was phenomenal.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: We were all there, Andy. You forgot your lines halfway through.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Exactly! Method acting. Showing vulnerability. The director totally got that. He was nodding. I saw him nod.

jokes.text: Andy? [silence]
jokes.setup: Andy asking what role he's playing
jokes.punchline: The uncomfortable silence as reality sets in
jokes.explanation: Masterful use of silence. The pause IS the punchline - perfect mockumentary timing.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: What role am I playing?

jokes.text: Two comps for m'lady and her Gabe.
jokes.setup: Andy giving tickets to Erin
jokes.punchline: Referring to Gabe as a possession/accessory with medieval language
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay treating Gabe like an object. Good character voice with theatrical pretension.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Erin, I got you tickets to the Cirque du Soleil.

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: Oh my God, thank you so much!

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: You can bring Gabe. He's like my squire.

jokes.text: Women cannot resist a man singing show tunes. It's so powerful, even a lot of men can't resist a man singing show tunes.
jokes.setup: Andy's plan to win back Erin through musical performance
jokes.punchline: Escalating the claim to absurd levels about show tunes' universal appeal
jokes.explanation: Good escalation structure. Very Andy-specific delusion about his theatrical appeal.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Erin, I'm going to win you back with the power of show tunes. They're scientifically proven to make people fall in love.

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: Really? Science?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Yes. In fact, studies show that show tunes increase dopamine by 47%. And if you listen to them backwards, you can hear messages from your soulmate.

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: Wow.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Plus, show tunes are the only thing that can cure cancer. Also, they make you taller. And more attractive. And they solve global warming.

jokes.text: That's awful. Everyone's gonna miss that guy.
jokes.setup: Learning Gabe can't come to the play
jokes.punchline: Sarcastic sympathy for universally disliked character
jokes.explanation: Classic Jim deadpan sarcasm. Plays on established character dynamics.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Oh no, Gabe can't make it? That's terrible. Who will make everyone uncomfortable and ruin the vibe?

jokes.text: What kind of terrible rumor monger is Gabe? He is woefully ill informed, all right?
jokes.setup: Gabe saying no one was going to the play
jokes.punchline: Andy's defensive overreaction with unnecessarily fancy language
jokes.explanation: Andy's theatrical vocabulary in mundane situation. Character-consistent pompousness.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Well, I think you'll find that the parameters of my thespian endeavor have been misconstrued. The theatrical presentation in question is not merely a play, but rather a sophisticated exploration of interpersonal dynamics rendered through the medium of dramatic performance.

jokes.text: Last time I went to the theater, A man dressed as a cat sat on my lap.
jokes.setup: Dwight explaining why he won't go to theater
jokes.punchline: Bizarre, specific traumatic theater experience
jokes.explanation: Perfectly Dwight - specific, weird, delivered matter-of-factly. High quotability and originality.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I will not be attending the theater. When I was nine years old, I was forced to watch a production of 'Our Town' in the round. The actors were inches from my face. I could see up their noses. One actor sneezed directly into my mouth. I have not been to a theater since.

jokes.text: They had to bring in a ringer. This guy's, like, world class.
jokes.setup: Andy consoling Michael about not getting the lead role
jokes.punchline: Sets up the later reveal that it's actually Darryl's plumber
jokes.explanation: Good setup for later payoff about the 'world-class' actor being a plumber. Works better on rewatch.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Michael, you're a great guy. You'll get the next role.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: You think so?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Yeah, I mean, look at Darryl. He got the lead in the school play.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Wait, Darryl's in the play?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Yeah, he plays the plumber.

jokes.text: You're that friend. I'm going to see your play.
jokes.setup: Michael saying he has plans to see a friend in Sweeney Todd
jokes.punchline: The dawning realization that Andy is that friend
jokes.explanation: Good comedic revelation structure. Michael's obliviousness played well.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I'm going to see Sweeney Todd this weekend. My friend invited me.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Oh, that's great! Which friend?

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Just... a friend.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Well, who?

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: You know what, it's you. You're my friend. You invited me to Sweeney Todd.

jokes.text: Noishe! And scene.
jokes.setup: Emotional moment between Michael and Andy
jokes.punchline: Michael treating real life like a theatrical exercise
jokes.explanation: Michael's theatrical pretension breaking the fourth wall of the moment. Good character beat.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Andy, I want you to know that you mean a lot to me. You're like the son I never had.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: That means so much, Michael. Thank you.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Now, let's really feel this moment. On three, we're both going to cry. One, two... *gets visibly emotional* ...this is real acting, Andy. This is Meisner technique.

jokes.text: Angela? Don't like her anymore. Not attracted to her anymore. Just contractually obligated to make a baby with her.
jokes.setup: Dwight being asked to disrobe
jokes.punchline: Treating reproduction like a business contract
jokes.explanation: Peak Dwight - completely matter-of-fact about absurd arrangement. Dark humor with perfect character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I have a pre-nup for my sperm. No emotional attachment, strictly business terms. The woman signs in triplicate.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Conception occurs on a schedule I've optimized for maximum efficiency. Forty-five minutes, including paperwork.

jokes.text: Well, there's a lot of gray area in that clause. Do you want to re-mediate?
jokes.setup: Dwight saying theater attendance isn't in their contract
jokes.punchline: Angela treating their relationship like actual contract law
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy - Angela's uptight nature applied to bizarre situation. Legal terminology humor.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Theater attendance isn't in our contract.

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: Then by that logic, our relationship is void. I'm filing for dissolution of partnership, effective immediately.

jokes.text: My parents used to scramble to find babysitters So they could take my little brother to do stuff.
jokes.setup: Jim understanding the babysitting struggle
jokes.punchline: Dark implication about favoritism and family dynamics
jokes.explanation: Subtle dark humor about childhood neglect. Jim's matter-of-fact delivery works.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Yeah, I get it. You watch your niece for like two hours and suddenly you're the favorite uncle. But then you realize your brother's been doing this every single day for years, and you're still somehow winning. That's when you know something's wrong with your family.

jokes.text: Maybe I have a niece my family never told me about.
jokes.setup: Looking for babysitting solutions
jokes.punchline: Absurd hope for unknown family member
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist logic. Jim's desperation leading to silly hope.

jokes.text: Neiche!
jokes.setup: Talking about having a niece
jokes.punchline: Theatrical pronunciation/celebration of the word 'niece'
jokes.explanation: Unexpectedly theatrical Jim moment. One word joke with high economy and quotability.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I have a niece.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Niece. Niece. NIECE!

jokes.text: I did, because I am being the bigger man, And balloons are bigger than flowers.
jokes.setup: Michael bringing balloons to theater
jokes.punchline: Literal interpretation of 'being the bigger man'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Michael logic - literal minded interpretation of idiom. Good absurdist reasoning.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I'm going to be the bigger man here and let this go.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: That's why I brought these balloons to fill myself up with helium.

jokes.text: Are you the guy that did an entire law & order episode For his audition?
jokes.setup: Someone recognizing Michael at the theater
jokes.punchline: Reference to Michael's over-the-top audition style
jokes.explanation: Good callback to Michael's theatrical pretensions. Sets up his reputation in local theater.

dialogue.speaker: Michael Scott
dialogue.line: Oh hey! You recognize me from the theater?

dialogue.speaker: Other person
dialogue.line: No, I recognize you from when you auditioned for our community production last year.

dialogue.speaker: Michael Scott
dialogue.line: Oh! Well, how did I do?

dialogue.speaker: Other person
dialogue.line: You didn't get the part. We felt your interpretation of a tree was a little... too much.

jokes.text: I've been trying to get in the babysitting game forever. The 13-year-olds in this town Have a complete monopoly. It's almost like a babysitters' club.
jokes.setup: Erin explaining why she wanted to babysit
jokes.punchline: Reference to 'The Babysitters Club' books/monopoly
jokes.explanation: Pop culture reference with slight wordplay. Erin's childlike perspective on adult problems.

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: I loved The Baby-Sitters Club books growing up. I thought if I became a babysitter, I could start my own club and we'd run the babysitting business like a monopoly.

jokes.text: Hey, I think this guy playing sweeney Todd is my plumber.
jokes.setup: The 'world-class' actor Andy mentioned
jokes.punchline: Revelation that the lead is actually Darryl's plumber
jokes.explanation: Excellent payoff to earlier setup. Punctures Andy's pretension perfectly. Great ironic reversal.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: So I've been doing some research on this world-class actor for the role...

dialogue.speaker: Darryl
dialogue.line: Yeah? Who'd you find?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: His name is... well, he's actually my plumber.

dialogue.speaker: Darryl
dialogue.line: Your plumber? Andy, that's my plumber.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Wait, what?

dialogue.speaker: Darryl
dialogue.line: Yeah, he fixed my sink last month. Guy's talented, I'll give him that, but world-class actor? Come on.

jokes.text: This guy's a world-class actor. He doesn't daylight as your plumber.
jokes.setup: Darryl identifying the lead actor as his plumber
jokes.punchline: Andy's continued delusion about the actor's credentials
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy - Andy can't accept reality. 'Daylight' as verb is nice touch.

dialogue.speaker: Darryl
dialogue.line: That's my plumber.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: What? No, that's a famous actor.

dialogue.speaker: Darryl
dialogue.line: No, that's definitely my plumber. He fixed my toilet last month.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Well, he probably does plumbing on the side. You know, between takes on his hit TV show.

jokes.text: Apparently, the director discovered him doing karaoke. It's his first play. He didn't even audition.
jokes.setup: The 'world-class' actor discussion
jokes.punchline: Multiple revelations making Andy's rejection more embarrassing
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation - each detail makes Andy's situation worse. Good structure building humiliation.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I was rejected from Juilliard.

dialogue.speaker: Darryl
dialogue.line: Juilliard? That's like the Harvard of acting.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, they rejected me.

dialogue.speaker: Darryl
dialogue.line: Wow. So you're not world-class?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I'm... I'm still a great actor.

dialogue.speaker: Darryl
dialogue.line: Yeah, but Juilliard said no.

jokes.text: If we don't listen to the overture, We won't recognize the musical themes When they come back later.
jokes.setup: People talking during theater performance
jokes.punchline: Oscar's pretentious theater knowledge
jokes.explanation: Classic Oscar pretension. Character-accurate but predictable humor.

dialogue.speaker: Oscar
dialogue.line: This is Chekhov. He's one of the greatest playwrights of all time. The depth, the nuance, the way he captures the human condition... Most people wouldn't understand it.

jokes.text: Excuse me, I think you may be in the wrong seats.
jokes.setup: The office workers at the theater
jokes.punchline: Implication they look out of place or are acting inappropriately
jokes.explanation: Standard 'fish out of water' humor. Functional but not memorable.

dialogue.speaker: Unknown theatergoer
dialogue.line: Excuse me, are you... are you guys okay? This is a Chekhov play.

jokes.text: I work with that guy.
jokes.setup: Andy singing on stage
jokes.punchline: Mundane workplace context contrasting with theatrical performance
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective contrast between theatrical and mundane. Good economy.

dialogue.speaker: Andy Bernard
dialogue.line: I'm performing a song from Sweeney Todd!

dialogue.speaker: Dwight Schrute
dialogue.line: That's not how you do Broadway.

dialogue.speaker: Jim Halpert
dialogue.line: Andy, you're in an office. No one cares.

dialogue.speaker: Andy Bernard
dialogue.line: You guys just don't appreciate real talent when you see it.

jokes.text: There's gum on the seat, And now it's on my work skirt. I have to go change. Too bad I'm not gonna explain anything that you miss.
jokes.setup: Angela dealing with gum on theater seat
jokes.punchline: Passive-aggressive threat about missing plot details
jokes.explanation: Very Angela - turning minor inconvenience into dramatic punishment. Good character voice.

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: Excuse me, I found gum on my seat. This is completely unacceptable.

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: Whoever did this, I hope you miss the entire plot of the movie. I hope you have to leave early and you never find out what happens.

jokes.text: His name's Andy. He's a terrible salesman.
jokes.setup: Andy performing romantic song on stage
jokes.punchline: Dwight's matter-of-fact professional assessment during emotional performance
jokes.explanation: Perfect Dwight - completely missing emotional context, focusing on sales performance. Good economy.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: That man is singing off-key.

jokes.text: Michael! Shelby Thomas weems, the director. I promise you That if you keep auditioning With a similar gusto... We are going to find a production With a role for Michael Scott.
jokes.setup: Michael being consoled by theater director
jokes.punchline: Backhanded compliment about his 'gusto' rather than talent
jokes.explanation: Good cringe moment - polite rejection disguised as encouragement. Slightly verbose.

dialogue.speaker: Theater Director
dialogue.line: You know, Michael, you have a lot of gusto.

dialogue.speaker: Michael Scott
dialogue.line: Thank you.

dialogue.speaker: Theater Director
dialogue.line: It's a shame you have no talent.

jokes.text: Why are you dressed like a seed catalog model? These are just my dirty old gardening clothes. They were all that I had in my car.
jokes.setup: Dwight's appearance at the theater
jokes.punchline: Angela's specific insult and Dwight's practical explanation
jokes.explanation: 'Seed catalog model' is a great specific insult. Both characters' voices come through clearly.

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: My God, Dwight. You look like a scarecrow had a baby with a beet.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: That's impossible. Scarecrows are not biologically compatible with beets. However, I have been working on the farm since dawn, and I did wear my work clothes.

jokes.text: No, no, don't put cece on the phone, Because she can't talk yet.
jokes.setup: Erin offering to put baby on phone
jokes.punchline: Pam's patient explanation of why babies can't have phone conversations
jokes.explanation: Simple observational humor about parenting reality. Pam's patient mothering voice.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Babies can't really talk on the phone, Erin. They don't have the language skills yet.

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: Oh, I know that. But he could listen!

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Well, he wouldn't understand what we're saying.

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: So? Neither do I half the time, and I still take calls.

jokes.text: Just checking my emails. See if I got any last-minute 'break a legs' Or 'I still love you' type texts.
jokes.setup: Andy on his phone during performance
jokes.punchline: Revealing his desperate hope for validation, especially from Erin
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Andy's insecurity and hope. Relatable desperation.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Hello? Yeah, I'm at work right now. Can you call me back in like five minutes? I'm in the middle of my performance review. Well, actually, it's not really a performance review, it's more like... they're just telling me stuff. But yeah, call me back. I want to hear about your day. I really do.

jokes.text: Unfortunately, in this ham-fisted production Of sweeney Todd, The real terror comes from the vocal performances.
jokes.setup: Oscar writing a review during the performance
jokes.punchline: Pretentious, harsh critique delivered matter-of-factly
jokes.explanation: Perfect Oscar pretension. 'Ham-fisted' is great vocabulary choice. Very character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: Oscar
dialogue.line: This is community theater at its worst. The blocking is amateurish, the staging is pedestrian, and whoever directed this clearly has no understanding of Stanislavski or basic dramatic theory. The lead actor is technically competent but emotionally vacant. And that set design? I've seen more sophisticated work in a high school gymnasium. Which, granted, is where this is being performed.

jokes.text: There's a little bird uttering around. Do hope it ceases chirping.
jokes.setup: Phone ringing during performance
jokes.punchline: Andy staying in character while addressing the interruption
jokes.explanation: Good theatrical improvisation. Andy's commitment to character during disruption.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, I'm so sorry. My phone is ringing. This is so embarrassing. I'm gonna take this call, but I'm gonna stay in character the entire time.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Hello? Hi, Mom. No, no, I'm in the middle of a performance. Yes, I know you told me to call you back. Mom, I'm literally on stage right now. Can I call you back? No, Mom, I can't talk about this now!

jokes.text: Oh, it appears the bird was in mine own pocket This whole time. He's gone to sleep now. I closed his beak.
jokes.setup: Andy's phone was the interruption
jokes.punchline: Elaborate theatrical metaphor for turning off his phone
jokes.explanation: Great commitment to theatrical metaphor. Very Andy - turning embarrassment into performance.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: You know what? I'm gonna turn off my phone. It's like... it's like Hamlet at the end of the play. He's had enough. He's done. The curtain falls. Lights out. No more texts, no more calls, no more... performance. Just silence. Beautiful, golden silence.

jokes.text: I've killed it! I'm a murderer! Just like you, sweeney Todd! See? It all connects.
jokes.setup: Andy's phone/bird metaphor
jokes.punchline: Taking the metaphor to absurd extremes, connecting to play's theme
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation and commitment. Andy's theatrical mind making wild connections. Peak character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: You know, relationships are like birds. If you hold them too tight, they die. If you let them go, they fly away.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: So what you're saying is we should all just be ornithologists?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: No, Dwight, that's not—

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I've started building nests in my apartment. Just in case.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Jim has seventeen bird feeders now.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: That's... that's not what I meant at all.

jokes.text: Not that I know you're a murderer. My character doesn't know that yet. But I'm suspicious, Because of all the razors That you have laying around.
jokes.setup: Andy breaking character about plot knowledge
jokes.punchline: Meta-commentary on dramatic irony while staying somewhat in character
jokes.explanation: Great meta-theatrical humor. Andy's confusion about character knowledge vs. audience knowledge.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Wait, wait, wait. I know what's about to happen here. I've seen this episode before... I mean, I haven't seen it, but I know I'm going to mess this up somehow because that's literally my entire character arc.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: The writers have made it very clear that whenever something important happens, I'm either not there, or I show up at the wrong time, or I accidentally sabotage myself. It's like they planned this.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Which is ironic because I'm trying so hard not to be that guy, but the show won't let me. So really, I'm a victim here.

jokes.text: Sounds like cece.
jokes.setup: Baby crying in theater audience
jokes.punchline: Jim's parental instinct recognizing his own baby's cry
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy - three words capture parental recognition. Relatable parent humor.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: That's my baby.

jokes.text: I just didn't think you guys were gonna see us. We were just gonna stop by and then get some ice cream And then go home.
jokes.setup: Erin caught bringing the baby to theater
jokes.punchline: Her naive explanation making the situation worse
jokes.explanation: Good Erin naivety. Her childlike logic making adult situation worse.

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: I didn't think it was a problem! The movie theater has a section for babies.

dialogue.speaker: Manager/Coworker
dialogue.line: Erin, that's the crying section in the back for people on their phones.

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: Well the baby was crying AND on a phone, so it was perfect!

jokes.text: Babies shouldn't have ice cream, by the way.
jokes.setup: Erin's plan to get ice cream with baby
jokes.punchline: Jim's practical parenting knowledge amid the chaos
jokes.explanation: Good deadpan delivery. Jim's parenting instincts amid the crisis.

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: I'm taking the baby for ice cream!

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Erin, the baby can't eat ice cream.

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: Well, maybe just a lick.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: No licks. The baby's like three months old.

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: So... chocolate or vanilla?

jokes.text: Well, we're never leaving the house again. Not together.
jokes.setup: The babysitting disaster
jokes.punchline: Pam's dramatic conclusion about future social life
jokes.explanation: Relatable new parent despair. Good escalation from one bad experience to permanent isolation.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: I can't believe I actually thought I could handle watching three kids for one evening. I mean, what was I thinking? By the end of the night, one of them had gotten into the finger paints, another one was using my good throw pillows as a fort, and the third one... I'm still not entirely sure where the third one went.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: So that's it. I'm done. No more babysitting, no more helping out, no more being the responsible one. From now on, I'm staying home on Friday nights, watching Netflix, eating ice cream straight from the container, and slowly accepting that this is my life now. This is who I am. I'm a hermit.

jokes.text: That was more horrifying than nunsense. All that singing got in the way Of some perfectly good murders.
jokes.setup: Reviewing the musical performance
jokes.punchline: Creed's disturbing preference for violence over music
jokes.explanation: Peak Creed darkness. Perfect character voice - preferring murders to musical numbers. High originality.

dialogue.speaker: Creed
dialogue.line: That was beautiful. You know, I'd kill to hear that again.

jokes.text: Mmm! Fruit is so much better when it's dried. I've already eaten, like, 30 apricots.
jokes.setup: Kevin at the theater reception
jokes.punchline: His innocent overeating and mathematical confusion
jokes.explanation: Classic Kevin food obsession. Simple but character-appropriate humor.

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: I don't understand. There's a lot of popcorn here, and I've eaten most of it. How is that possible?

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: I did the math. There's no way one person can eat this much popcorn.

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: Unless... there's more than one of me.

jokes.text: How's the toilet? Silent.
jokes.setup: Talking to the plumber who played Sweeney Todd
jokes.punchline: Professional plumbing talk contrasting with theatrical performance
jokes.explanation: Great economy and contrast. Perfect callback to the plumber reveal. 'Silent' works as both plumbing and theatrical term.

dialogue.speaker: Darryl
dialogue.line: So you're telling me you were in Sweeney Todd?

dialogue.speaker: Plumber
dialogue.line: Yeah, I played the barber. It was off-Broadway.

dialogue.speaker: Darryl
dialogue.line: That's cool. So when you're not slitting throats on stage, you're fixing pipes?

dialogue.speaker: Plumber
dialogue.line: Exactly. The pipes don't slit themselves.

jokes.text: This plumber has pipes.
jokes.setup: Complimenting the plumber's singing
jokes.punchline: Double meaning of 'pipes' - plumbing and vocal cords
jokes.explanation: Excellent pun with perfect double meaning. High economy and quotability.

dialogue.speaker: Darryl
dialogue.line: Man, you got some pipes on you! That voice is incredible.

dialogue.speaker: Plumber
dialogue.line: Thanks, I appreciate that.

dialogue.speaker: Darryl
dialogue.line: No, seriously. With pipes like those, you should be on a stage, not fixing pipes.

jokes.text: You didn't have to boo him. Well, he was getting a lot of applause, And I just didn't think it was indicative Of how people were really feeling.
jokes.setup: Michael booing the plumber actor
jokes.punchline: Michael's twisted logic about correcting false positive reception
jokes.explanation: Great Michael logic - positioning rudeness as public service. Good character psychology.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: No, no, no. You see, if I boo him now, and then later it turns out he's a good plumber, I'll look like an idiot. But if I don't boo him, and it turns out he's a bad plumber, then I'll look like an idiot. So really, the smart thing to do is boo him now, get it out of the way.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: That's not how any of this works.

jokes.text: Boo! I appreciate the feedback.
jokes.setup: Jim demonstrating how Michael would feel being booed
jokes.punchline: Michael's polite acceptance contrasting with his own booing
jokes.explanation: Good reversal showing Michael's hypocrisy. Nice economy in the exchange.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: You'd be like, 'Oh, well, thank you for the feedback. I appreciate your honesty.'

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: That's not true.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: And then I'd be like, 'Booo! Booo! Get off the stage!'

jokes.text: Okay, kill me! Just kill me! Kill me! Ah, blood everywhere.
jokes.setup: Angela and Dwight's role-playing scenario
jokes.punchline: Uptight Angela enthusiastically acting out violent death
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character contrast - prim Angela doing theatrical violence. Very funny juxtaposition.

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: Oh my God, yes! Finally! FINALLY! Oh, that feels so good!

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: More! More! Faster! Harder! YES! YES! YES!

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: Oh God, I'm gonna... I'm gonna...

jokes.text: Contractually, we're obligated to have sex. Well, I won't tell if you don't. I will tell. I will tell the mediator.
jokes.setup: Angela trying to avoid their contractual obligation
jokes.punchline: Dwight's commitment to contract enforcement over romance
jokes.explanation: Perfect Dwight - treating intimacy like business contract. High originality and character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: Dwight, we can't keep doing this. It's wrong.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Angela, we have a signed contract. Section 3, subsection B clearly states our obligations.

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: I don't care about the contract. This has to stop.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: A contract is a binding legal document. My feelings are irrelevant. We continue until the terms are fulfilled or you face breach of contract penalties.

jokes.text: That's broth, Gabe.
jokes.setup: Gabe asking for soup while sick
jokes.punchline: Erin's matter-of-fact correction about liquid food categories
jokes.explanation: Simple observational humor. Erin's literal-minded correction. Good economy.

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: That's not soup, Gabe. Soup is a food. That's a beverage.

jokes.text: I'll see you soup. [giggles]
jokes.setup: Saying goodbye to Gabe
jokes.punchline: Wordplay on 'see you soon' and 'soup'
jokes.explanation: Simple pun with Erin's characteristic giggle. Fits her childlike humor style.

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: See you soon!

dialogue.speaker: Gabe
dialogue.line: Actually, I'm leaving the company.

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: Well, see you in soup!

jokes.text: I think we have, like, a gift bottle of irish cream. Yeah? We could put that in orange juice. Get it. Yeah? Let's get our juice on.
jokes.setup: Jim and Pam dealing with their disastrous evening
jokes.punchline: Makeshift cocktail solution and Jim's enthusiasm for it
jokes.explanation: Relatable parent desperation humor. 'Get our juice on' is mildly amusing but not exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: The restaurant lost our reservation, the babysitter canceled, and I spilled wine all over my dress.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Hey, hey, look at me. We can fix this.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I'll make us cocktails.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: With what? We don't have any liquor.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I found a bottle of vanilla extract, some old cooking wine, and what I'm pretty sure is moonshine that Creed gave me.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Jim, no.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Trust me. I'm calling it 'The Dunder Mifflin Special.'

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: One sip and you'll forget all about tonight.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Because I'll be in a coma?

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Exactly! It's foolproof.

jokes.text: I booed someone tonight. I have no filter. And if I thought you were terrible, I would have booed.
jokes.setup: Michael trying to compliment Andy
jokes.punchline: Using his rudeness as proof of honesty
jokes.explanation: Good Michael logic - using bad behavior as credibility. Nice callback structure.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Andy, I want you to know that I think you're a great guy. And I mean that sincerely.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Thanks, Michael.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: No, really. Because if I didn't think you were great, I would tell you. I would just say it. I would be really rude about it.

jokes.text: But I thought that you were exactly awesome. No better, no worse.
jokes.setup: Michael trying to give Andy a genuine compliment
jokes.punchline: Precise measurement of awesomeness undermining the compliment
jokes.explanation: Perfect Michael - trying to be nice but making it worse. 'Exactly awesome' is a great phrase.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Andy, you're like 87% awesome.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: 87%? That's oddly specific.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I did the math.

jokes.text: I did not say that to make you feel good. I just said it. Total integrity.
jokes.setup: Andy thanking Michael for the compliment
jokes.punchline: Michael clarifying his lack of emotional intent
jokes.explanation: Michael accidentally making compliment worse by explaining his honesty. Good character consistency.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Thank you, Michael. That means a lot coming from you.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Oh, I wasn't trying to be nice. I was just stating a fact.

jokes.text: In the criminal justice system, The people are represented by two separate But equally important groups, The police who investigate the crimes
jokes.setup: End of episode callback
jokes.punchline: Michael doing Law & Order opening as mentioned earlier
jokes.explanation: Good callback to earlier setup about Michael's audition style. Nice bookend structure.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: In the criminal justice system, sexually-based offenses are considered especially heinous. In New York City, the dedicated detectives who investigate these vicious felonies are members of an elite squad known as the Special Victims Unit. These are their stories.

tmdb_overview: Andy invites the entire office to his community theater production of Sweeney Todd in hopes that Erin will attend. Michael tries to put his jealousy aside and enjoy the performance while Jim and Pam struggle with their incompetent babysitter.
insight: Andy's community theater debut carries the episode on character comedy, light on plot payoff.
